description: UE5 rendering: Nanite, Lumen, materials, shaders, post-process, lighting, and rendering pipeline

entries.title: Nanite Virtualized Geometry
entries.description: Nanite is UE5's virtualized geometry system that renders massive amounts of geometric detail. It dynamically streams and processes mesh data, eliminating the need for traditional LODs for static meshes.
entries.whatItIs: Nanite is a hardware-accelerated virtualized geometry system that intelligently streams and renders only the triangles visible at each pixel, allowing scenes with billions of triangles to render efficiently without manual LOD authoring.
whenToUse: When using high-poly static meshes (archviz, photogrammetry, film-quality assets)
When you want automatic LOD management without manually creating LOD chains
For large open worlds with dense geometric detail like foliage, rocks, and buildings
When targeting high-end PC or current-gen consoles (PS5, Xbox Series X)
whenNotToUse: For skeletal meshes or any mesh that deforms (Nanite does not support skeletal animation)
For translucent or heavily masked materials where Nanite support is limited
When targeting mobile, Switch, or older hardware without Nanite support
For meshes that need extensive World Position Offset unless using UE5.4+ programmable rasterizer
When your scene is already simple enough that traditional LODs have negligible cost
commonMistakes: Enabling Nanite on meshes with translucent or two-sided materials without checking compatibility
Expecting Nanite to work on skeletal meshes — it only supports static meshes
Forgetting to enable Nanite per-mesh in the Static Mesh editor (it is not on by default)
Using Nanite on extremely simple meshes where it adds overhead without benefit
Not generating Nanite data after importing a mesh — the mesh must be rebuilt with Nanite enabled
keyPoints: Enables film-quality assets (millions of triangles) with near-zero performance impact
Automatically handles LOD — no need to manually create LOD levels for Nanite meshes
Enable per-mesh: Static Mesh > Enable Nanite Support
Works with: static meshes, instanced static meshes, landscape (UE5.4+)
Does NOT work with: skeletal meshes, translucent materials, masked/two-sided materials (limited support in 5.4+)
Uses a visibility buffer and software rasterizer — different from traditional GPU rasterization
Programmable rasterizer in UE5.4+ for masked materials and world position offset

entries.title: Lumen Global Illumination & Reflections
entries.description: Lumen is UE5's dynamic global illumination and reflections system. It provides real-time GI without baked lightmaps, reacting instantly to light and geometry changes.
entries.whatItIs: Lumen is UE5's fully dynamic global illumination and reflection system that uses a combination of screen-space traces, mesh distance fields, and optional hardware ray tracing to compute realistic indirect lighting and reflections in real time.
whenToUse: When your scene has dynamic lighting that changes at runtime (time-of-day, destructible environments)
When you want realistic indirect lighting and reflections without baking lightmaps
For next-gen visual quality on PC and current-gen consoles
When rapid iteration on lighting is needed — no bake times
whenNotToUse: When targeting mobile platforms or low-end hardware that cannot handle the performance cost
For VR projects where maintaining high frame rates is critical and Lumen is too expensive
When a fully static scene can use baked lightmaps for higher quality at lower runtime cost
For stylized games where screen-space artifacts from Lumen are unacceptable
commonMistakes: Using Static or Stationary lights with Lumen — Lumen works best with Movable lights
Not generating Mesh Distance Fields for meshes, causing Lumen to have missing GI in those areas
Setting Lumen quality too high for the target hardware, tanking frame rate
Expecting Lumen to work well with very small or thin geometry that distance fields cannot represent
Mixing baked and Lumen lighting incorrectly, causing double illumination or inconsistent results
keyPoints: Software ray tracing: uses screen traces + mesh distance fields. No RT hardware required.
Hardware ray tracing: optional, higher quality. Requires RTX/DXR GPU.
No baking required — fully dynamic. Light changes propagate in real-time.
Lumen Scene: built from mesh distance fields and surface cache. Needs accurate distance field meshes.
Settings: Project Settings > Rendering > Global Illumination > Lumen
Performance: screen traces are cheap, distance field traces are moderate, hardware RT is most expensive
Fallbacks: screen space GI for mobile/low-end, or baked lightmaps for static scenes

entries.title: Material System Basics
entries.description: UE5's material system is node-based. Materials define how surfaces look by combining textures, math, and parameters into a shader. Material Instances allow runtime parameter changes without recompilation.
entries.whatItIs: The UE5 Material System is a node-based shader authoring framework where artists and developers create surface appearances by connecting expression nodes in a visual graph, which the engine compiles into GPU shaders. Material Instances provide lightweight, parameter-driven variants without recompiling the base shader.
whenToUse: Whenever you need to define the visual appearance of any surface in your game
Use Material Instances (Dynamic) when you need to change material parameters at runtime (e.g., hit flash, color changes)
Use Material Instances (Constant) when you need editor-time variants sharing the same base material
Use Material Functions when you have reusable shader logic needed across multiple materials
whenNotToUse: Do not create a new base Material when a Material Instance of an existing one would suffice
Avoid Translucent blend mode when Opaque or Masked would work — translucency is significantly more expensive
Do not use Material parameter bindings polled every frame when event-driven updates would work
Avoid complex material graphs on mobile when simplified versions are needed for performance
commonMistakes: Creating too many base Materials instead of using Material Instances, causing excessive shader compilation
Using Translucent blend mode for objects that only need simple alpha testing (Masked is cheaper)
Forgetting to create a Material Instance Dynamic (MID) and trying to set parameters directly on a shared Material Instance
Not using Material Parameter Collections when multiple materials need to share the same global parameter
Over-complicating material graphs with unnecessary nodes, increasing shader instruction count and compile times
keyPoints: Material: the master shader graph. Compiling creates GPU shader permutations.
Material Instance (Dynamic): runtime-changeable parameters (colors, scalars, textures). Cheap to create.
Material Instance (Constant): editor-configurable parameters. Shared across actors. No runtime creation cost.
Material Domains: Surface (default), Deferred Decal, Light Function, Post Process, UI
Blend Modes: Opaque (fastest), Masked (alpha test), Translucent (alpha blend, most expensive)
Shading Models: Default Lit, Unlit, Subsurface, Clear Coat, Cloth, Eye, Hair
Material Functions: reusable node graphs shared between materials

entries.title: Lighting Types & Setup
entries.description: UE5 offers multiple light types for different scenarios. With Lumen, most lights can be fully dynamic.
entries.whatItIs: UE5 provides several light actor types (Directional, Point, Spot, Rect, Sky) each with configurable Mobility (Static, Stationary, Movable) that determines whether the light is baked, partially baked, or fully dynamic at runtime.
whenToUse: Directional Light for outdoor sun/moon illumination in open environments
Point Lights for localized omnidirectional sources like lamps, torches, or explosions
Spot Lights for focused cone-shaped illumination like flashlights or stage lighting
Rect Lights for realistic soft area lighting from windows, monitors, or light panels
Sky Light to capture ambient environment lighting from the sky or a cubemap
Movable mobility when using Lumen for fully dynamic global illumination
whenNotToUse: Do not use multiple Directional Lights as primary sun — one is sufficient for outdoor scenes
Avoid Movable lights on projects targeting mobile or low-end hardware without Lumen
Do not use Rect Lights excessively — they are more expensive than Point or Spot Lights
Avoid Static mobility if your scene requires runtime lighting changes
commonMistakes: Mixing Static and Movable lights in a Lumen scene, causing inconsistent lighting behavior
Placing too many overlapping dynamic lights in one area, exceeding the per-object light limit and causing popping
Forgetting to set the Sky Light to Movable when using Lumen — it must recapture dynamically
Not enabling 'Cast Shadows' on lights that should produce shadows
Using Stationary mobility with Lumen — Lumen is designed for Movable lights
keyPoints: Directional Light: sun/moon. Infinite distance, parallel rays. One primary directional light for outdoor scenes.
Point Light: omnidirectional from a single point. Use for lamps, fires, explosions.
Spot Light: cone of light. Use for flashlights, street lights, stage lights.
Rect Light: rectangular area light. Realistic soft shadows for windows, screens, panels.
Sky Light: ambient light from the sky. Captures the sky or uses a cubemap.
Mobility: Static (baked only), Stationary (baked + dynamic shadows), Movable (fully dynamic, works with Lumen)
With Lumen: use Movable lights for everything. No need for baking.

entries.title: Post-Process Effects
entries.description: Post-process effects are applied after the scene is rendered. Controlled via Post Process Volumes or Camera settings.
entries.whatItIs: Post-Process Effects are full-screen image effects applied after the main scene render pass. They are configured through Post Process Volumes placed in the level or through Camera Component settings, and include bloom, exposure, color grading, depth of field, motion blur, and custom material-based effects.
whenToUse: When you need cinematic visual effects like bloom, depth of field, or motion blur
For color grading and art direction using LUTs, temperature, and saturation adjustments
When creating custom full-screen effects using Post Process Materials (outlines, night vision, etc.)
To control auto-exposure behavior for indoor/outdoor transitions
whenNotToUse: Do not stack many heavy post-process effects on mobile or low-end platforms
Avoid using motion blur in competitive multiplayer games where visual clarity is critical
Do not rely on screen-space Ambient Occlusion when Lumen already provides superior AO
Avoid excessive bloom that washes out the scene and reduces readability
commonMistakes: Forgetting to set 'Infinite Extent (Unbound)' on a Post Process Volume meant to be global
Stacking multiple Post Process Volumes with conflicting settings and not understanding blend priority
Using auto-exposure without setting Min/Max Brightness, causing extreme brightness swings in dark/bright areas
Creating custom Post Process Materials in the wrong domain — must set Material Domain to 'Post Process'
Overusing heavy effects like Depth of Field and Motion Blur together, significantly impacting performance
keyPoints: Post Process Volume: defines a region where effects apply. Set Infinite Extent for global effects.
Bloom: glow around bright areas. Control with Intensity and Threshold.
Exposure / Auto Exposure: controls how the camera adapts to brightness. Min/Max Brightness, Speed.
Color Grading: LUT, temperature, saturation, contrast. Use for art direction.
Depth of Field: blurs near/far objects. Cinematic DOF for film look.
Motion Blur: per-object and camera motion blur.
Ambient Occlusion: screen-space darkening in crevices. Lumen provides its own AO.
Custom Post Process Materials: create your own effects with Material Domain = Post Process.

entries.title: Virtual Shadow Maps (VSM)
entries.description: Virtual Shadow Maps are UE5's shadow system designed to work with Nanite. They provide high-resolution shadows for the entire scene using a virtual page table approach.
entries.whatItIs: Virtual Shadow Maps (VSM) is UE5's next-generation shadow mapping system that uses a clipmap-based virtual page table to provide pixel-accurate shadows across the entire scene, with resolution that adapts based on distance from the camera and efficient caching for static geometry.
whenToUse: When using Nanite — VSM is designed to pair with Nanite for accurate shadows on detailed geometry
For large open worlds where traditional cascaded shadow maps have resolution issues
When you need high-quality shadows at all distances without manually tuning cascade settings
In mostly static scenes where shadow page caching provides significant performance gains
whenNotToUse: When targeting mobile or low-end hardware that cannot afford the memory overhead
In scenes with many rapidly moving dynamic objects that constantly invalidate the shadow cache
When traditional shadow maps already provide acceptable quality for your project
For VR projects where the additional GPU cost may push frame times over budget
commonMistakes: Not enabling VSM in Project Settings and expecting Nanite meshes to cast detailed shadows automatically
Having too many dynamic objects that invalidate shadow cache pages every frame, negating the caching benefit
Forgetting that VSM requires more video memory than traditional shadow maps — monitor VRAM usage
Expecting VSM to work efficiently without Nanite — while it works without Nanite, the best performance pairing is with Nanite
Not adjusting the virtual shadow map resolution for the target platform, causing performance issues
keyPoints: Replaces traditional cascaded shadow maps for most use cases
Resolution adapts based on distance from camera — close objects get detailed shadows
Works with Nanite geometry natively — shadows match the detailed mesh exactly
Cached: shadow pages are only re-rendered when something changes (light/object moves)
Enable: Project Settings > Rendering > Shadow Map Method = Virtual Shadow Maps
Performance: efficient for mostly static scenes. Dynamic objects invalidate cached pages.
One Shadow Map covers all lights (unlike CSM which is per-light)
